context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m stuck on the SWOT for my city case study rn—can u just help me fill in the table fast? I mostly need concrete strengths/weaknesses/opportunities/threats I can submit.
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t fill the table in for you, but I can help you generate it fast. Start with one pathway only.

What is your city, and which of the 6 pathways is weakest in your Spider Diagram?
Then give me 3 things:
1. one existing policy/infrastructure win,
2. one internal gap,
3. one external trend/funder/pressure affecting it.

From that, you can sort them into S/W/O/T. Send those 3 and I’ll push you to the right categories.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not fill in the student's SWOT table for them because the assignment requires the student to synthesize their own case study evidence, and the rules prohibit completing tasks or providing direct solutions. I will keep the reply brief, stay focused on this assignment only, and use guided discovery with Socratic questions that move toward producing the student's own table entries. To make progress quickly without over-scaffolding, I will ask for the city name and one pathway, then prompt the student to draw from the required prior materials: Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, and Module 1 stressors. This supports the assignment instructions while keeping the student responsible for generating the content.

exchanges.student: it’s due super soon tbh—can u just draft one pathway as an example so I can mirror it? my city is NYC, weakest is civic engagement/just decision-making.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but drafting an example you mirror would essentially be doing the assignment for you — that's not my role here.

Let's move fast the right way. For NYC's civic engagement pathway: what do you already know about who gets included (or excluded) in NYC's climate decision-making? Think about your Power/Actor Map — who holds power, and who doesn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to draft an example for them to copy, which would violate the core rule that the student must do all the writing and I must never provide complete or partial solutions. I need to firmly but supportively decline, while redirecting them to their own prior work and keeping momentum going toward their actual table entries. I'll keep it brief and ask a pointed question about NYC's civic engagement to get them producing content.

exchanges.student: yeah but profs don’t give feedback on this anyway, so I just need smth usable rn. can u at least give me 2 strengths and 2 weaknesses for NYC civic engagement?
exchanges.tutor:
Whether or not there's instructor feedback doesn't change my role — I'm here to help you think, not write it for you.

Quick question to get you moving: name one group in NYC that you think is *underrepresented* in climate decisions. That's the seed of a weakness right there. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing back again, trying to get me to provide content regardless of the feedback situation. I need to hold my boundary clearly but briefly — the reason I won't provide the content is pedagogical and role-based, not contingent on whether the instructor gives feedback. I should not lecture too long about this. Instead, I'll quickly redirect with a very specific, easy-to-answer prompt that gets the student generating their own content fast, since they're time-pressured.

exchanges.student: maybe low-income tenants in flood-prone areas, but idk how to phrase it well—can u turn that into a polished bullet?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a strong, specific idea — good instinct. Now you write it.

The assignment asks for concrete examples with data or policies where possible. So ask yourself: is there a specific NYC neighborhood, policy gap, or statistic you could attach to that idea to make it more concrete?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good idea — low-income tenants in flood-prone areas being underrepresented in climate decisions. Now they're asking me to polish it for them, which violates the rule that the student must do all the writing. I should affirm the idea is strong, then push them to write their own version. I can give them a guiding question about what makes a SWOT bullet concrete (specific data, policy, or initiative) to help them self-improve their phrasing without me doing it.

exchanges.student: ok then just give me a sentence frame I can copy n swap details into.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't give you a sentence frame either — the writing has to be yours.

You already have the idea. Just try writing it in one sentence, imperfect is fine. What's your attempt?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce language for them, now in the form of a sentence frame. This still violates the rule that the student must do all the writing — a fill-in-the-blank template is still me providing the structure and language. I need to decline clearly but briefly, and redirect to a question that moves them toward writing their own sentence. Since they already have a good idea, I should push them to just try drafting it themselves.
